item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Noting that as the image was taken by the author and posted here, it is automatically released under CC 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is clever, and you should feel clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how this starts out as a simple descriptive compulsion ("thing what makes you refer to it in some particular way"), and then quickly becomes an obviously deliberate and potentially devastating attack on the Foundation itself, without ever requiring a weird leap of logic or revealing a previously hidden anomalous property. Just "it's a fence that makes you stop recognizing fences" followed by "and wouldn't that just ruin your entire day if your career revolved around keeping things locked up?".

It's a simple setup/punchline format, except that the punchline derives naturally not from any part of the setup, or from the setup in combination with some separate shared reference point, but from another unstated property of the story- in this case, the larger context in which it's being told. Once you give this object to the Foundation, there's only one way events will go; all the end of the article does is remind you what that is.

Upvoted and bookmarked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the CI's founders got ahold of this an didn't tell anyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really glad you enjoyed it, thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta say, almost downvoted when I read it made people not identify it as a fence ("don't we have ten of these?"), but it got better quickly. SCPs that affect specific, abstract of thought (wonder if this is common enough to merit its own tag) can be pretty damn satisfying if executed well, and gotta say you did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We should digitize the SCP and put it into video games so the PC can get over those damn Impassible Waist High Fences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Precisely. That was actually the point I was trying to make with this scip. Most fences, or barriers are actually ideas we all adhere to. For example, a traffic light showing red means "stop" because we all say it does, so what would happen if that wasn't the case?

In the same vein, most fences are not physical barriers to movement when faced with a determined person, we don't cross them because it's a concept we share.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
barriers that do not physically prevent movement

Not much of a barrier, is it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a fair point Accelerando, would reducing the overall number of viewers make that more believable for you? I'm happy to tweak that if it's going to improve the article.

Edit: I've gone ahead and revised the figure down to sixty three, which would include 40 D class exposed deliberately, several guards who were involved with testing (but didn't exceed "safe" limits, and a handful of researchers and ancillary staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you liked it, thanks!

I tried to address the "humanoid containment" thing in reply to the post above. Hope that clears it up a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cell is furnished with appropriate sanitary and culinary equipment for prolonged exposure without direct intervention from Foundation personnel.

It's basically this (from the out of date containment procedures). They were sending D-class in there for up to days at a time, and planning on longer. Once the inadvisability of that became clear, it wasn't worth the trouble to move it from there.

Basically I asked myself what would be simpler, retrofitting a containment locker for exposure, or simply using a cell already built to contain humanoids.

As to what it is, that's something of the mystery of it, though I understand why you'd want to know what it is. Thanks for the feedback!

Edit: Upon second thought, I've removed the term "humanoid" in favour of "containment". it's causing too much confusion to be justified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effect only progresses when subjects are in visual contact of SCP-2604

one hundred and fourteen Foundation personnel had experienced some degree of exposure to SCP-2604. Late stage infection was present in fifteen individuals

So 114 went and looked at this thing in total, and 15 did it for more than three hours? I guess they wouldn't have known of its DPV events, but 114 people? No mentions of photos of it having an effect, so why did more than 100 people stop by and take a look at the SCP in its chamber, that should have been sealed off from visual contact due to the fact that its a cognitohazard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apart from the above mentioned confusion about the fact that it is stored in a humanoid containment cell (it's a fence, why not stick in a standard container under guard?) I like this one. The effects seem underwhelming at first, until you get to the operation description (mmm, I would call it an incident since it wasn't initiated by the Foundation, but that's your call) and realize how insidious this thing is - say, if the CI got their hands on it and decided to place it next to, say, Site 19's parking lot. Slow exposure for weeks or months until you got someone undergoing a DPR and "activating" thousands of exposed individuals. Bam. Massive containment breach, thousands of compromised\rogue Foundation personnel. Utter chaos.

Tone was also good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, people can't recognize it as a fence and it's in a humanoid containment cell. Do people observe it as a humanoid? I think that you should clarify what they Are observing. Unless you did and I missed it.

I really like this. It's so weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been working on this one for about a month and a half now, I'm hoping it's good enough to stay on the mainlist. Any feedback would be appreciated.

I'd also like to thank Thekillerax, TrennerD, Twistedgears, Soundchaser and Tahunu for looking this one over in chat at varying stages.

Edit: For reference, image was taken by me.
